texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A novel membrane vacuum regeneration (MVR) technology was developed to achieve lower regeneration energy demand for CO2 absorption process. Sixteen different amine-based absorbents including primary, secondary, tertiary amines, polyamines, diamines, and sterically hindered amines were analyzed by combined absorption-desorption screening experiments for selecting the most effective absorbents for CO2 membrane vacuum regeneration (MVR) technology. The CO2 absorption experiments were conducted in a hollow fiber membrane contactor (HFMC) at 25 1 C and 12.5 kPa CO2 partial pressure. The CO2 rich solvent was regenerated at 75 1 C and 20 kPa regeneration pressure by using the MVR technology. The chain length, number of functional groups, side chain, and number of hydroxyl groups of amines were investigated to get a better understanding on the effects of the structure in the MVR process. The results show that the tertiary amines have greater regeneration performances than the primary amines, but the CO2 absorption rates of the tertiary amines are worse than the primary amines. For the polyamines, the absorption performance of polyamines can be improved by increasing the number of amine groups while the MVR performance of the polyamines can be reduced. Steric hindered amines exhibited greater MVR performances. In addition, amines with steric hindrance effect caused by the substitution of hydroxyl group or methyl group to amine group in structure has also shown good performance in the MVR process for CO2 desorption. The overall performance of amine-based absorbents was also investigated by an absorption-desorption characters analysis. It shows triethylenetetramine (TETA) and N -methyldiethanolamine (MDEA) have great average CO2 absorption rates and relatively high cyclic capacities per mole absorbent, and ethylenediamine (EDA) is considered to be the promising absorbent as cyclic capacity in mol CO2/kg absorbent. Diethanolamine (DEA) and 2-Amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (AMP) are found to have the best overall performances in terms of CO2 absorption rate at lean loading and cyclic capacity in mol CO2/mol absorbent. In terms of overall performance, MDEA is mostly recommended for using in MVR process. Sweeping steam can significantly improve MVR performance in most selected absorbents except AMP and 2-amino-2-hydroxymethyl-1,3- propanediol (THAM) absorbents which present the potential to reduce the regeneration energy demand by operating without sweeping steam. The study also finds that MVR shows better regeneration performance as compared to traditional thermal regeneration process at the same regeneration temperature.

texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CO2 absorption process based on amine-solvents is considered to be the most promising choice for CO2 separation from flue gas. The advantages of the process includes the flexibility to lower CO2 partial pressure gas streams, higher purity of CO2 product and the tail-end technology which can be installed on existing power plant directly. Due to these advantages, the process has been successfully commercialized in many power plants (Rao and Rubin, 2002; Rocelle, 2009; Aaron and Tsouris, 2005; Davison et al., 2001). Nevertheless, the main challenge of the process is its high energy penalty for absorbents regeneration which is also the main concern for many power plants (Abu-Zahra et al., 2007; Alie et al., 2005). In conventional MEA-based CO2 capture process, it requires over 120 1 C steam extracted from LP to provide heat for CO2 stripping, the energy demand of the stripping can reduce 9-15% of power output from a power plant (Xu et al., 2010). Thus, the development of the low energy requirement in a stripping process has been proposed recently.
texts.text: CO2 absorption process based on amine-solvents is considered to be the most promising choice for CO2 separation from flue gas. The advantages of the process includes the flexibility to lower CO2 partial pressure gas streams, higher purity of CO2 product and the tail-end technology which can be installed on existing power plant directly. Due to these advantages, the process has been successfully commercialized in many power plants (Rao and Rubin, 2002; Rocelle, 2009; Aaron and Tsouris, 2005; Davison et al., 2001). Nevertheless, the main challenge of the process is its high energy penalty for absorbents regeneration which is also the main concern for many power plants (Abu-Zahra et al., 2007; Alie et al., 2005). In conventional MEA-based CO2 capture process, it requires over 120 1 C steam extracted from LP to provide heat for CO2 stripping, the energy demand of the stripping can reduce 9-15% of power output from a power plant (Xu et al., 2010). Thus, the development of the low energy requirement in a stripping process has been proposed recently.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Van Wagener and Rochelle (Van Wagener and Rochelle, 2011) have simulated five different configurations in a stripping process, including 1-stage flash, simple stripper, stripper with adiabatic lean flash, 2-stage flash and interheated column. They found that the best configuration is the interheated column which can save 9-11% energy demand. The improvements were attributed to the better reversibility of more complex flowsheets. Similar work has also been reported by Oyenekan and Rochelle (2007). They have evaluated four innovative configurations in the stripping process and have found that the matrix stripper shows the best performance. Although these studies demonstrated that heat duty can be reduced by improvement of stripper configurations, all of them are expensive. In addition, these configurations are still restricted to high regeneration temperatures which could result in problems such as thermal degradation and volatile loss of absorbents.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Alternatives using membrane contactor combined with pressure swing technology to replace conventionally thermal CO2 desorption process are widely reported recently (Teramoto et al., 2003; Teramoto et al., 2004; Okabe et al., 2009; Simioni et al., 2011; Koonaphapdeelert et al., 2009; Kosaraju et al., 2005; Fang et al., 2007). This technology has the potential to reduce the energy requirement for CO2 desorption by introducing a low-temperature regeneration (e.g. 343 K) concept. The energy for the low-temperature regeneration can be introduced from low-grade heat or waste heat rather than the high-grade steam from the steam cycle (Gibbins et al., 2005). As the low-temperature regeneration results in low reaction kinetics, technologies such as vacuum or inert gas sweeping have been widely used to improve the CO2 desorption kinetics by increasing the driving force of the mass transfer. Koonaphapdeelert et al. (2009) used pure N2 gas as the sweep gas to strip CO2 from rich MEA solution at 80100 1 C in ceramic hollow fiber membrane contactors. Simioni et al. (2011) also investigated membrane stripping of CO2 from aqueous potassium solvent using PTFE membrane and asymmetric PS membrane with hydrophobic surface modification. Both membranes were tested at stripping temperatures from 60 to 100 1 C and nitrogen (N2) was used as the sweeping gas to improve the CO2 desorption kinetics. Fang et al. (2007) have studied the regeneration of CO2-rich MEA solution at low temperature by using the vacuum technology in the shell side of a hydrophobic polypropylene (PP) hollow fiber membrane contactor (HFMC). However, most studies using MVR technology were based on MEA solution, a range of absorbents have not been investigated yet. As the CO2 vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) characteristics and reaction activities in different absorbents are various, it is important to determine suitable CO2 absorbents for MVR process, the relationships between absorbents structure and MVR performance have been reported in our previous work (Yan et al., 2012). Eleven typical absorbents and absorbents with one hydrogen on amine group and two or more hydroxyl groups on their structures were recommended due to their compressive performances in regeneration. However, previous work was focused on the regeneration characters of the absorbents. The absorption characters after regeneration have not been considered yet. In order to find the promising absorbents for MVR technology, it is important to investigate the overall performance of absorbents in absorption -desorption processes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This work aims to find appropriate absorbents with high potential for low energy requirement in CO2 MVR process in a combined absorption-desorption processes. Sixteen different amine-based absorbents were analyzed by using rapid screening experiments. The selection criteria of the absorbents are CO2 cyclic capacity and CO2 absorption rate. The chain length, number of functional groups, side chains, and number of hydroxyl groups of amines were also investigated to get a better understanding of the relationship between molecular structure and the performance in the MVR process. Typical amines such as monoethanolamine (MEA), diethanolamine (DEA), triethanolamine (TEA) and N -methyldiethanolamine (MDEA) were selected in this study. The effect of carbon chain length and side chains on amines and diamines such as hexamethylenediamine (HDA), 3-amino-1propanol (3-AP), 2-Amino-1-butanol (2-AB) and EDA,1-amino-2propanol (AP) and 1,2-propanediamine (PDA) were investigated by using the MVR technology. The effects of amine groups in polyamines such as ethylenediamine (EDA), diethylenetriamine (DETA), triethylenetetramine (TETA) and tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA) were also investigated in the study. Sterically hindered amines with different numbers of hydroxyl groups including 2-Amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (AMP), 2-amino-2-ethyl-1,3-propanediol (AEPD) and 2-amino-2-hydroxymethyl-1,3-propanediol (THAM) were compared to investigate the effects of hydroxyl groups.

Meanwhile, two gas steam inputs, 12.5% CO2 and 87.5% N2 (Hangzhou Jingong Gas Co., Ltd., purity 4 99.9%) by volume were mixed by mass flow controllers with a total gas flow of 2 L/min at ambient pressure. The mixed steam was saturated by bubbling into a pre-wet vessel and then flowed into the shell side of HFMC to react with solvents. The CO2 absorbed solvent was sent back to the stirring vessel and mixed well with fresh one for continuous cycle absorption, and the outlet CO2 concentration in gas steam was monitored by FTIR gas analyzer (GASMET FTIR Dx4000). When the concentration of CO2 in outlet reached 95% of inlet CO2 concentration, this test ended considering that the solvent has almost reached equilibrium and has no effective absorption capacity anymore.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Subsequently, the CO2-rich solution was then transferred into the rich solvent reservoir of CO2 membrane vacuum regeneration apparatus (b) to continue the desorption test. In this test, the CO2-rich solution was continuously pumped into the tube side of the HFMC with a flow rate of 2.5 ml/min and then heated to regeneration temperature of 75 1 C before the inlet of membrane module. The liquid stream temperatures and pressures were metered in both inlet and outlet of membrane module. The pressure in shell side of membrane contactor was kept at 20 kPa absolute by pressure regulating valve of vacuum pump. Hence, the CO2 would permeate the gas-filled membrane pores to the shell side and be regenerated from the rich solution due to the positive effects of reduced pressure. Meanwhile, in order to further strengthen regeneration process, the low temperature (75 1 C) water steam coming from the steam generator flowed through the shell side of HFMC with a flow rate of 0.5 g/min and acted as the sweeping gas to reduce the CO2 partial pressure in gas phase and provide higher mass transfer driving force. Then the CO2 and sweeping steam were extracted together from the shell side of membrane contactor into the condenser in which water steam was condensed. Finally, the CO2 was enriched and could be collected from the vacuum pump. Each experiment lasted for 1 h to make sure that the steady state is reached. In addition, it should be noted that additional steam sweeping would lead to extra heat for regeneration, thus operating mode without sweeping steam was conducted as well for seeking the trade-off between energy cost and improvement of regeneration performance.

But in terms of CO2 lean loading after MVR, it was 0.357 mol/mol for MEA which was obviously higher than 0.241 mol/mol for DEA as steam sweeping took place. texts.orig: In the tertiary amines (TEA and MDEA) solvents, CO2 can only form bicarbonate in the absorption process according to the Eqs. 8 and 9 (Versteeg and van Swaaij 1988). In general, the reaction of CO2 hydration is much slower than the reaction to form a carbamate. Thus, as illustrated in Fig. 2, the absorption rates in TEA and MDEA solvents were much slower than in DEA and MEA solvents. It should be noticed that the absorption rate of TEA was slightly slower than MDEA, resulting from the weaker basicity in TEA (pKa ¼ 7.8) solution than in MDEA (pKa ¼ 8.52) (NIST 2001). In addition, the hydroxyl group substitution at TEA amine group can create steric hindrance effect at the reaction site and thus reduce the reactivity towards CO2. However, the TEA exhibited greater performance on desorption than other absorbents due to the steric hindrance effect. It has been found that the TEA solution can almost completely be regenerated in the MVR process with steam sweeping.

texts.orig: Fig. 3 also shows the effect of the number of amine groups on the polyamines structures on the MVR performance. With increasing amine groups, the lean CO2-loading of solvents after MVR increased as well. The corresponding absorption rates in lean CO2-loaded polyamine absorbents showed an ascending trend with the increase of amine groups on their structures. EDA with two amine groups, for example, had the initial CO2 absorption rate of 5.89E � 3 mol/min. After the MVR with sweeping steam, which was higher than 3.65E � 3 mol/min of DETA with three amine groups. Therefore, it can be concluded that a higher removal ratio of CO2 in the polyamine solutions can be achieved with less amine groups at the cyclic absorption-desorption process. In addition, the results showed that the sweeping steam presented a positive effect on improving the MVR performance for polyamine absorbent as more than 0.1 mol of CO2 per mole of amine was desorbed with the sweeping steam than that without sweeping steam.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Three steric hindered amines (AMP, AEPD and THAM) with different numbers of hydroxyl groups were selected to analyze their MVR behaviors. It should be noted that 15 wt% AEPD solvent was carried out since its carbamate has limited solubility in water at high CO2 loading condition at room temperature. As presented in Fig. 4, AMP exhibited the highest CO2 absorption rate and THAM exhibited the lowest absorption rate among all the amines. As the steric hindered amines react with CO2 to form carbamate, the phenomenon can be explained by the different basicities of steric hindered amines. It has been reported that the pKa of AMP, AEPD and THAM is 9.69, 8.80 and 8.10, respectively (NIST 2001). Therefore AMP presents stronger affinity to accept proton via deprotanated pathway in Eq. 6 and results in faster overall reaction rate with CO2. From the view of chemical structure, more hydroxyl group substitution at a carbon to amine groups can result in stronger steric hindrance effect which inhibits the CO2 reaction with amine group in absorbents. Therefore, THAM with three hydroxyl group substitutions presented the lowest CO2
texts.text: Three steric hindered amines (AMP, AEPD and THAM) with different numbers of hydroxyl groups were selected to analyze their MVR behaviors. It should be noted that 15 wt% AEPD solvent was carried out since its carbamate has limited solubility in water at high CO2 loading condition at room temperature. As presented in Fig. 4, AMP exhibited the highest CO2 absorption rate and THAM exhibited the lowest absorption rate among all the amines. As the steric hindered amines react with CO2 to form carbamate, the phenomenon can be explained by the different basicities of steric hindered amines. It has been reported that the pKa of AMP, AEPD and THAM is 9.69, 8.80 and 8.10, respectively (NIST 2001). Therefore AMP presents stronger affinity to accept proton via deprotanated pathway in Eq. 6 and results in faster overall reaction rate with CO2. From the view of chemical structure, more hydroxyl group substitution at a carbon to amine groups can result in stronger steric hindrance effect which inhibits the CO2 reaction with amine group in absorbents. Therefore, THAM with three hydroxyl group substitutions presented the lowest CO2

texts.label: text
texts.orig: absorption rate. These steric hindrance effects also decreased the rich CO2-loading by increasing the number of hydroxyl groups, thus AMP showed the highest rich CO2-loading.
texts.text: absorption rate. These steric hindrance effects also decreased the rich CO2-loading by increasing the number of hydroxyl groups, thus AMP showed the highest rich CO2-loading.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Due to the presence of steric hindrance effect, AMP, AEPD and THAM all show outstanding MVR performances. As shown in Fig. 4, after MVR with sweeping steam, rich AMP was regenerated from 0.444 mol/mol rich loading to the 0.267 mol/mol and the regeneration ratio was 40%. THAM showed greater performance with 71% regeneration ratio from 0.364 mol/mol rich loading to 0.100 mol/mol lean loading. It also can be found that lower lean CO2-loading of solvent can be achieved as the hydroxyl groups in the structure increase. This might be caused by the weakening of carbamate stability as the presented hydroxyl groups can facilitate to hydrolyze carbamate and form bicarbonate. The MVR performance of the steric hindered amines slightly decrease without steam sweeping. Thus, the CO2 desorption of the steric hindered amines in the MVR was mainly controlled by the kinetic reaction rather than mass transfer. Therefore, the absorbents have the potential to reduce the regeneration energy without decreasing CO2 absorption performance by abandoning sweeping steam.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Three primary alkanolamines with similar chemical structure of MEA were selected to compare with MEA for their MVR performance. 3-AP has longer carbon chain length (3) than MEA. 2-AB and AP have substitution of a hydroxyl group positioned at a -carbon and b -carbon. As they are all primary amines, the CO2 absorption rates were similar to each other at corresponding CO2loadings and shown as Fig. 5. Tiny differences can still be found in the amines. For instance, the absorption rates of MEA and 3-AP were faster than that of 2-AB and AP. This might be caused by the substitution of a hydroxyl group to amine groups. The substitution could result in steric hindrance effect on amine groups. The AP showed a faster CO2 absorption rate than 2-AB as the hydroxyl groups of AP substitute at b -carbon rather than at a -carbon which cause weaker steric hindrance effect acted on amine groups on AP structure.

texts.orig: Comparison of the MVR behaviors for the primary alkanolamines was also investigated in the study. 3-AP presented worst desorption performance with lean CO2-loading of 0.400 mol/mol as the sweeping steam was introduced. This is because the increase of carbamate stability resulted from the longer carbon chain length of 3-AP. In addition, the pKa of 3-AP is 9.96 which is higher than that of MEA (pKa ¼ 9.5) (NIST 2001). Therefore its carbamate is difficult to be hydrolyzed to form bicarbonate and results in higher lean loading than in MEA. The MVR performances of 2-AB and AP were 0.321 mol/mol and 0.351 mol/mol in lean loading solutions, respectively. The performance of the amines was better than MEA solution due to the effect of steric hindrance by substitution of hydroxyl groups. The sweeping steam has showed a significant positive influence on the improvement of CO2 desorption from rich absorbent for primary alkanolamines.

texts.orig: In order to make good selection decision on the most promising absorbents for MVR technology, analysis of a single character of absorbent is not convincing. Therefore, it is necessary to analyze the overall performance of absorbents by combined absorption-desorption characters. A quantifying criterion for selecting absorbents was presented by evaluating the CO2 cyclic capacity, MVR ratio, average CO2 absorption rate per cycle, CO2 absorption rate at lean loading and amount of CO2 absorbed per cycle, as shown in Tables 3 and 4.

texts.orig: An industrial and more interesting way to evaluate the absorbent is by investigating the CO2 absorption rate versus cyclic absorption capacity. A scatter diagram for average CO2 absorption rate against cyclic capacity is plotted in Fig. 7. Average absorption rate indicates the CO2 reaction rate which determines the residence time of absorbent in the absorber column. Higher cyclic capacity means more CO2 can be removed per cycle for an absorbent. In Fig. 7, the absorbents with the best performance appear at the top right hand corner. It shows that TETA had the potential to be the promising absorbent for MVR technology with steam sweeping. EDA had the highest average CO2 absorption rate, but its molar cyclic capacity is not excellent. MDEA presented the greatest cyclic capacity, but average CO2 absorption rate was slow. MEA, as the most widely used amine, appeared at the bottom left hand corner of plot, was not the promising absorbent for MVR technology

texts.text: 3.8. CO2 absorption rate at lean loading versus cyclic capacity

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CO2 absorption rate at lean loading is the fastest rate for absorbent to react with CO2 as absorbent return to absorber. Higher absorption rate at lean loading indicates the greater potential to get high CO2 removal efficiency as equilibrium achieved at the top of absorber. Fig. 9 shows the CO2 absorption rate at lean loading against cyclic capacity in mole CO2 per mole absorbent. In the case of sweeping steam, DEA exhibited greater overall performance than other absorbents, and EDA had the highest CO2 absorption rate at lean loading with moderate cyclic capacity per mole amine. As no sweeping steam was involved in MVR process, AMP showed greatest potential to be the promising absorbent.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.9. Sensitivities on sweeping steam for different amine-based absorbents
texts.text: 3.9. Sensitivities on sweeping steam for different amine-based absorbents

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The energy demand for MVR process mainly consists of three parts: the sensible heat to raise the solvent temperature to regeneration temperature, the electric power needed for vacuum pump and the low-temperature steam extracted from LP turbine. If the amount of sweeping steam could be minimized in MVR process, it will be of great potential to reduce the amount of lowtemperature steam extracted from LP turbine, and then reduce the energy demand for regeneration. Thus, sensitivities of different absorbents on sweeping steam in terms of cyclic capacity were investigated in Fig. 11. It shows that steam sweeping only had slight improvement on cyclic capacity increase for AMP and THAM, which both are steric hindrance amines. EDA was very sensitive to sweeping steam which could improve the cyclic capacity from 1.083 to 2.583 mol CO2/kg absorbent. Therefore, AMP and THAM are the absorbents having the potential to reduce the regeneration energy demand in MVR process by abandoning sweeping steam.

texts.orig: 3.10. Absorbent recommendation for MVR process
texts.text: 3.10. Absorbent recommendation for MVR process

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An evaluation criterion was erected to estimate which absorbent is the most suitable one for MVR. Practically, to select the most effective solvents for any process, one must consider not only absorption and desorption performance, but also cost of solvents, energy used for the process, operational problems that may associate with the use of those solvents such as solvent degradation/losses, foaming, toxicity and corrosion. But in this work, we will only recommend the solvents based on certain considerations which will not necessarily reflect the actual costeffective solvents. Five different aspects were considered, including average CO2 absorption rate, cyclic capacity in mol CO2/mol absorbent, cyclic capacity in mol CO2/kg absorbent, CO2 absorption rate at lean loading and amount of CO2 absorbed per cycle with no sweeping steam. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusion
texts.text: 4. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to find appropriate absorbents with high potential for low energy requirement in the MVR process, the combined absorption-desorption properties of 16 different amine-based absorbents were studied by rapid screening experiments. Moreover, the effects of chain length, number of functional groups, side chains and number of hydroxyl groups were also investigated to get a better understanding of the relationship between the structure and MVR performance. By the MVR performance analysis, the results showed that tertiary amines had greater regeneration behaviors but worse CO2 absorption rates than primary amines. For the polyamines, increasing the number of amine groups improved the absorption performance of polyamines but lowered MVR performance. As a result of the presence of steric hindrance effect, steric hindered amines exhibited greater MVR performance. The substitution of hydroxyl groups or methyl groups to amine groups can result in steric hindrance effect which has positive influence on the improvement of CO2 desorption from rich absorbent via MVR technology. The overall performance for amine-based absorbents was also investigated by the combined absorption-desorption characters. It showed TETA and MDEA had great average CO2 absorption rates and relatively high cyclic capacities per mole absorbent. EDA can be considered as a promising absorbent due to its superior cyclic capacity in mol CO2/kg absorbent. It has been found that DEA and AMP have better overall performance in terms of CO2 absorption rates at lean loading conditions and cyclic capacity in mol CO2/ mol absorbent. Sweeping steam had significant positive effect to improve MVR performance except AMP and THAM absorbents. In terms of overall performance, MDEA is mostly recommended for using in MVR process. The study has also found that the MVR had superior performance than thermal regeneration at similar regeneration condition.
